Changing the Jack's car rental to Gbike bicycle rental is just to put things in context. The instructor has no intention of earning credit for posing the problem. Credit goes to Sutton and Barto. 18 Solution - Lights-Out (4x4) - parts (A)-(D) Below I give a clean, course-ready answer for each part. I also use the exact initial board from and (row 3, col 4) (rows/cols counted from top-left = (1,1)). I'll mark 1 = ON, 0 = OFF. Initial state (4x4, row-major): Row\Col 1 2 3 4 1000 0000 0001 0000 1) (A) - Pose as a state-space search problem State representation: a 4x4 binary matrix (or a 16-bit vector) s where s[i, j] e {0, 1} (0 = OFF, 1 = ON). Total state space size = (2^{16}) Initial state: as above. Actions: click(r,c) for any cell (r,c) with 1 r,c 4.Applying click(r,c) toggles the state (0<>1) of the cell (r, c) and its orthogonal neighbours (r1, c) and (r, c1) (only those inside the board). Transition function: Result(s, click(r, c)) = new state with those 1-5 bits toggled. Goal test: all cells OFF (all zeros). Cost function: unit cost per click (cost = 1). (You can also consider uniform-cost if clicks had differing costs.) Search space properties: branching factor b = 16. Depth = number of clicks. Note: because. clicking twice on same cell cancels (mod 2), sequences can be considered modulo 2 there are linear dependencies (useful later).. 2) (B) Suggested heuristic(s) We want an admissible (never-overestimates) heuristic estimating the minimum number of clicks to reach all-off. Two simple heuristics: h0(s) = 0 - trivial admissible heuristic. h1(s) = ceil((#ON(s)) / 5) Reason: a single click affects at most 5 cells (center cell + 4 neighbours). So in the best case one click can switch off up

are linear dependencies (useful later).. 2) (B) Suggested heuristic(s) We want an admissible (never-overestimates) heuristic estimating the minimum number of clicks to reach all-off. Two simple heuristics: h0(s) = 0 - trivial admissible heuristic. h1(s) = ceil((#ON(s)) / 5) Reason: a single click affects at most 5 cells (center cell + 4 neighbours). So in the best case one click can switch off up to 5 currently-ON lights. Therefore the minimum number of clicks needed is at least cei1(k/5) where k = number of ON lights in s. Use h(s) = cei1(k/5). Since each grid unit = 10 km, define. h(n);=; 10\cdot\sqrt{(x_n-x_G)^2+(y_n-y_G)^2}\quad\text{(km)} where ((x_G,y_G)) are the coordinates of the goal node (G). Why this is admissible. Any real path from (n) to (G) must physically cover at least the straight-line distance between (n) and (G). Therefore the true shortest-path cost (h^(n)) satisfies (h^(n)\ge) straight-line distance. So (h(n)\le h^*(n)) for every (n) i.e. (h) never overestimates and is admissible. Why this is consistent (monotone). For any edge ((u,v)) with actual edge cost (c(u,v) (the distance to travel from (u) to ()), the Euclidean distances obey the triangle inequality: \text{dist}(u,G) \le \text{dist}(u,v) + \text{dist}(v,G) Multiplying by 10 (km per unit) gives h(u) \Ie c(u,v) + h(v) provided (c(u,v)) is at least the straight-line distance between (u) and (v) (which holds if edge costs represent geometric path lengths). Thus (h) is consistent. (If an edge cost were defined in some non-geometric way and could be smaller than the. straight-line distance, the admissibility/consistency argument would need the corresponding check; for the usual planar-graph edge-length interpretation the Euclidean heuristic is both. admissible and consistent.) (b) Will A* with this heuristic find an optimal path from S to G? Yes. For three nodes (A,B,C) with the same skeleton (edges (A!-!B) and (B!-!C), i.e. a chain), there. are four possible orientations: 1. (A\to B\to C) (chain) 2.(A\leftarrow B\rightarrow C) (fork: (B)>both) 3.(A\leftarrow B\leftarrow C) (chain reversed) 4.(A\to B\leftarrow C) (collider / v-structure at (B)) Which are I-equivalent? The three DAGs (1), (2), (3) do not contain a collider (X\to B\leftarrow Y); they share the same skeleton and have no v-structure. Hence they are I-equivalent to each other. The DAG (4) has a v-structure at (B) and thus belongs to a different Markov. equivalence class. It is not I-equivalent to the other three. Breadth-First Search (BFS) Breadth-First Search (BFS) is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores the vertices of a graph layer by layer. It is widely used in various applications, such as finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph, determining connectivity, and building spanning trees. Overview Given a graph G=(V,E)G = (V, E)G=(V,E) and a distinguished source vertex sss, BFS systematically explores the edges of GGG to:. 1. Discover all reachable vertices from sss.. 2. Compute the shortest distance (in terms of the number of edges) from sss to each reachable vertex. 3. Construct a breadth-first tree rooted at sss, where the path from sss to any vertex vvv corresponds to the shortest path in GGG. Key Concepts 1. Layered Exploration: BFS expands the "frontier" of discovered vertices evenly across layers. Vertices at distance kkk from sss are fully explored before any vertex at distance k+1k+1k+1 is processed. 2. Vertex Colors: BFS uses three colors to track the state of each vertex: oWhite: Undiscovered vertices. o Gray: Discovered vertices with unexplored neighbors. o Black: Fully explored vertices. 3.Breadth-First Tree: o As BFS discovers vertices, it builds a tree with sss as the root. o Each vertex vvv is connected to its predecessor uuu, the vertex that first discovered vvv. [CS203] Design and Analysis of Algorithms Course Instructor: Dr. Dibyendu Roy Autumn 2024-25 Scribed by: Your name (202351130) Lecture (Week 5) 1 Introduction Quicksort is a highly efficient sorting algorithm based on partitioning an array into smaller sub- arrays. Here’s a brief overview of how it works... Quicksort is a highly efficient divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm. It works by selecting a pivot element from the array, then partitioning the array into two subarrays: one with elements smaller than or equal to the pivot and the other with elements greater than the pivot. The algorithm recursively sorts the subarrays and, in the process, sorts the entire array. 1.1 Description of Quicksort Quicksort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm, similar to merge sort, but more efficient in practice. It uses the divide-and-conquer approach to sort arrays. Here’s a breakdown of the three steps involved in the process for sorting a subarray A[p... r]: A. Divide In this step, the array is partitioned into two subarrays: • Choose a pivot element, typically A[r], the last element in the array. • Rearrange the elements so that: – All elements in the subarray A[p... q− 1] are less than or equal to the pivot A[q]. – All elements in the subarray A[q + 1... r] are greater than the pivot. By doing this, the pivot is in its correct sorted position. The index q is computed during the partitioning process, which divides the array into two smaller parts. Example: For an array A = [9, 3, 7, 1, 8], selecting 8 as the pivot would lead to: • Left subarray: [3, 7, 1] • Right subarray: [9] With the pivot 8 placed between them. B. Conquer After partitioning, the algorithm recursively sortsthe two subarrays: • The subarray A[p... q− 1] (elements smaller than or equal to the pivot). • The subarray A[q + 1... r] (elements larger than the pivot). The recursion continues until the subarrays contain one

Combine In quicksort, no explicit combination step is required because the array is already sorted once the recursive calls return. The pivot naturally divides the sorted parts, so the entire array A[p... r] is now sorted. 2. Quicksort Implementation QUICKSORT(A, p, r) 1 if p < r 2 q = PARTITION(A, p, r) 3 QUICKSORT(A, p, q - 1) 4 QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, r) To sort an entire array A, the initial call is QUICKSORT(A, 1, A.length()). 2.1 Partitioning the Array The key to the algorithm is the PARTITION procedure, which rearranges the subarray A[p... r] in place. PARTITION(A, p, r) 1 x = A[r] # Choose the pivot as the last element 2 i = p - 1 # i is the boundary of the "less than or equal to pivot" partition 3 for j in range(p, r): 4 if A[j] <= x: 5 i += 1 6 A[i], A[j] = A[j], A[i] # Swap elements to move them to the left partition 7 A[i + 1], A[r] = A[r], A[i + 1] # Place the pivot in its correct position 8 return i + 1 # Return the pivot index The partition procedure is the key part of quicksort. It rearranges the elements of the subarray A[p... r] such that elements smaller than the pivot are placed on one side and those larger on the other. This is achieved by iterating through the array and swapping elements based on comparisons with the

Time Complexity Analysis The average case time complexity of Quicksort is O(n log n), where n is the number of elements in the array. This is due to the logarithmic number of partitions and linear time for each partition. However, in the worst case (when the smallest or largest element is always chosen as the pivot), the time complexity degrades to O(n2). Randomly selecting a pivot or using the median-of-three rule can help mitigate this risk and improve performance in practice. 5. Conclusion Quicksort is an efficient and widely used sorting algorithm that performs well in average cases. It’s essential to understand the mechanics of the partitioning process and how it contributes to the algorithm’s performance.

[CS203] Design and Analysis of Algorithms Course Instructor: Dr. Dibyendu Roy Autumn 2024-25 Scribed by: Shivam Kumar (202351130) Lecture (Week 5) 1 Dynamic Programming (DP) Dynamic Programming (DP) is a method used for solving complex problems by breaking them down into simpler subproblems. It is applicable when the subproblems overlap, meaning the same subproblems are solved multiple times. The main idea is to store the results of these subproblems to avoid redundant calculations. 1.1 Key Concepts A. Optimal Substructure: A problem exhibits optimal substructure if an optimal solution can be constructed from optimal solutions of its sub- problems. B. Overlapping Subproblems: A problem has overlapping subproblems if the same subproblems are solved multiple times. C. Memoization vs. T abulation: • Memoization (T op-Down): Recursively solves subproblems and caches their results. • T abulation (Bottom-Up):Iteratively solves subproblems and stores results in a table. 1.2 Key Steps in Dynamic Programming When developing a dynamic programming algorithm, we typically follow these four steps: • A. Characterize the Structure of an Optimal Solution. • B. Recursively Define the V alue of an Optimal Solution. • C. Compute the V alue of an Optimal Solution. • D. Construct an Optimal Solution. 2 Rod Cutting Problem The rod cutting problem is a classic example of dynamic programming. 2.1 Scenario Serling Enterprises buys long steel rods and wants to cut them into shorter pieces to sell, aiming to maximize profit. 2.2 Prices For a rod of length i inches, there is a price pi in dollars. 2.3 Objective Given a rod of length n inches and a list of prices p1, p2,..., pn, the task is to find the maximum revenue rn. 2.4 Example A. Length of Rod: Consider a rod of length n = 4. B. Cutting Options: Options include no cuts or combinations of cuts. C. Revenue Calculation: For a rod of length 4, the maximum revenue can be determined. 2.5 Key Point There are

Pseudocode for Bottom-Up Approach: BOTTOM-UP-CUT-ROD(p, n) 1 let r[0...n] be a new array 2 r[0] = 0 3 for j = 1 to n 4 q = -1 5 for i = 1 to j 6 q = max(q, p[i] + r[j - i]) 7 r[j] = q 8 return r[n] 5.3 Comparison of Approaches Both approaches have the same time complexity of O(n2). 6 EXTENDED-BOTTOM-UP-CUT-ROD This algorithm computes both the maximum revenue and the first piece of length that leads to that revenue. functional blocks:. 1. Resistive Respiration Sensor Interface 2. Analog Front-End (Signal Conditioning) 3. Microcontroller Unit (MCU) 4. Power Management Unit 3. Resistive Respiration Sensor Interface. A two-pin connector (J_SENsOR1) is used to interface the external resistive respiration sensor. SENsOR+: Connected to the excitation and measurement path. SENsOR-: Connected to system ground Breathing causes small resistance changes in the sensor, which are converted into voltage. variations by the analog front-end. 4. Analog Front-End (AFE) 4.1 Constant Current Source (U2A) Op-Amp: TLV9062 (Unit A) Function: Generates a stable constant current through the resistive sensor .R_SET (33 kQ): Sets the excitation current value This approach ensures that sensor resistance variations are linearly converted into voltage changes. 4.2 Signal Amplifier (U2B) Op-Amp: TLV9062 (Unit B) Function: Amplifies the small voltage variations from the sensor R_G (10 kQ) & R_F (100 kQ): Configure the amplifier gain This stage improves signal amplitude while maintaining low noise performance 4.3 ADC Input Filtering Before feeding the signal to the MCU ADC: :R1 (100 Q): Series resistor :C2 (100 nF): Shunt capacitor to ground Together, they form a low-pass Rc filter that suppresses high-frequency noise and stabilizes ADC sampling. Output net: ADC IN 5. Microcontroller Unit (MCU) 5.1 ESP32-C3-WROOM-02 The ESP32-C3 serves as the central controller and provides: 12-bit ADC for analog signal acquisition Digital processing and filtering Wireless communication (Bluetooth / Wi-Fi). 5.2 Supporting Components .C1 (0.1 F): Local decoupling capacitor R3 (10 kQ): EN pin pull-up resistor The conditioned analog signal is sampled through the ADc_IN pin.. 6. Power Management Unit 6.1 Power Input J2: 5V input from USB or external supply. 6.2 Voltage Regulation U3 (AMS1117-3.3): Linear regulator providing 3.3V. C4, C5 (10 F): Bulk capacitors for stability .C6, C7 (0.1 F): High-frequency decoupling capacitors. This ensures a stable and low-noise supply for both analog and digital circuits. 7. Grounding Strategy A common ground (GND1) is used across analog, digital, and power sections. Short ground paths

System software is a collection of computer programs that control a computer's hardware computer's hardware and the user, allowing users to interact with the computer's software and hardware. [1, 2, 3] System software includes: [1] Operating system (Os): The most well-known example of system software, the OS manages all other programs on the computer. It allocates and organizes resources based on priority, and tracks all processes occurring in the system. [1, 4] Device drivers: A component of system software. [5] Utility software: Programs that help with the management and maintenance of the computer system. Examples include anti-virus, disk management tools, and data backup tools. The multiplicative inverse map (x\mapsto x^{-1}) (in GF(2^8)) is nonlinear: it does not satisfy (S(x\oplus y)=S(x)\oplus S(y)) in general. A simple counterexample using known S-box values:. AES S-box value (S(0)=0x63) AES S-box value (S(1)=0x7C). Now (1\oplus1=0). If the S-box were linear over XOR, we would have (S(1\oplus1)=S(1)\oplus S(1)). But (S(0)=0x63), whereas (S(1)\oplus S(1)=0x7C\oplus0x7C=0). These are different, so the S-box is not linear. (More broadly: multiplicative inversion is not a linear map over the vector space GF(2)^8.) Maximum efficiency = 36.8% (almost double that of Pure ALOHA) Summary: By synchronizing transmissions to time slots, Slotted ALOHA reduces collisions by half, resulting in higher efficiency. A broadcast channel allows multiple nodes to communicate over a shared medium. . Challenges: Avoid collisions and coordinate who transmits when.. Ideal Multiple Access Protocol:. o Fair for all nodes Avoids idle time Simple and decentralized Efficient utilization of bandwidth. 1.2 Types of Multiple Access Protocols 1. Channel Partitioning Protocols: TDmA (Time Division Multiple Access): Each node transmits in its own time. slot. FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access): Each node has a dedicated frequency band. CDmA (Code Division Multiple Access): Each node has a unique code; multiple nodes transmit simultaneously. 2. Random Access Protocols: Pure ALOHA: Transmit anytime; vulnerable time = 2 x frame time; efficiency = 18.4%. Slotted ALOHA: Transmit at beginning of time slot; vulnerable time = frame time; efficiency = 36.8%. oCSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access): Listen before transmitting to avoid collisions.. Variants: Type Behavior 1-persistent Transmit immediately if channel idle Non-persisten Wait random time if channel busy P-persistent Transmit with probability P in a slot O-persistent Transmission order assigned by supervisor CSMA/CD: Collision Detection used in Ethernet. Stop transmitting if collision occurs. CSMA/CA: Collision Avoidance used in Wireless (WiFi IEEE 802.11). Uses RTS/CTS to avoid hidden and exposed terminal problems.. 1.3 Hidden & Exposed Terminal Problems. Hidden Terminal: Two nodes can't sense each other; collision occurs at receiver. Exposed Terminal: Node refrains from sending even if it wouldn't interfere Solution: MACA / MACAW protocols using RTS/CTS and ACKs. Module 2: Flow Control and ARQ Protocols.

Example Problem: Frames: 11, Window Size: 4, Lost Frame: 5th. Solution Steps: 1. Send frames 1-4 -> 4 frames sent 2. Send 5th frame -> 5 frames sent 3. Send 6th frame after ACK 1 > 6 frames sent 4. Plaintext space (P={a,b}) with (\Pr[a]=\Pr[b]=\tfrac12) Key space (K={K_1,K_2,K_3}) with (\Pr[K_1]=\tfrac12,\\Pr[K_2]=\tfrac14,\\Pr[K_3]=\tfrac14)] Ciphertext space (C={1,2,3,4}). Encryption mapping: \begin{aligned} \operatorname{Enc}(a,K_3)=2,\ &\operatorname{Enc}(b,K_1)=3,\quad \operatorname{Enc}(b,K_2)=3,\quad \operatorname{Enc}(b,K_3)=4 \end{aligned} Definition (perfect secrecy): For every plaintext (m) and ciphertext (c) with (\Pr[C=c]>0), \Pr[M=m\mid C=c] =\Pr[M=m] Compute marginals and a posteriori:. First compute (\Pr[C=1]): \Pr[C=1] =\Pr[M=a]\Pr[C=1\mid M=a] +\Pr[M=b]\Pr[C=1\mid M=b] \Pr[C=1] = \tfrac12\cdot\tfrac12 + \tfrac12\cdot 0 = \tfrac14. Now compute the posterior (\Pr[M=a\mid C=1]) via Bayes:. \Pr[M=a\mid C=1] =\frac{\Pr[C=1\mid M=a]\Pr[M=a]}{\Pr[C=1]} = \frac{\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac12}{\tfrac14} = 1. equality required for perfect secrecy fails.. Conclusion: (loperatorname{Enc}) does not provide perfect secrecy (observation of ciphertext (1) reveals plaintext (a) with certainty).. Remark (general criterion): For (possibly non-uniform) key distributions, perfect secrecy requires for every ciphertext (c) and all plaintexts (m,m'):. \sum_{k:\operatorname{Enc}(m,k)=c}\Pr[K=k] \sum_{k:\operatorname{Enc}(m',k)=c}\Pr[K=k]. Keys in DBMS Primary Key: Uniquely identifies a record. (Cannot be NULL.) .Candidate Key: All possible keys that can be primary keys Super Key: Any combination of attributes that uniquely identifies a row. Integrity Constraints Entity Integrity: No NULL values in primary key.. Referential Integrity: Foreign key value must exist in referenced table. A minimal set of FDs that preserve the original dependency set.. Should have:. Proof Using the Pumping Lemma: 1.Assume L is regular: Assume that L-{ a^n3 I n1 } is a regular language.. 2. Pumping length p: Let p be the pumping length as guaranteed by the Pumping Lemma. 3. Choose a string s E L: Choose a string s=ak^3 e L, where k is chosen 4. Split the string: According to the Pumping Lemma, we split s-xyz, where: : Ixyl p and Iyl>0. Pump the string: Now, we will pump the string by choosing different values ofi. oFor i=0: Remove y from the string, resulting in xz-a^(p3-k) - The new string has a length of p3-k, which is no longer a perfect cube. Hence, xz L. o For i=2: Double y, resulting in xy'z=a^(p'+k) - The new string has a length of p'+k, which is not a perfect cube either, as p'+k does not form a cube number. Hence, xy?z L. 6. Contradiction: In both cases, the pumped strings xz and xy'z do not belong to the language L, because their lengths are no longer perfect cubes. This contradicts the condition that the pumped string must still belong to the language L. 7. Conclusion: Since pumping the string leads to a contradiction, the assumption that L is regular is false. SRTF vs SJF Equivalence Statement: Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) behaves like Shortest Job First (SJF) if the. set of processes is fixed and all processes are CPU bound. Answer:. True. Justification:. When there are no new arrivals and all processes are known in advance, SRTF always picks. the process with the least remaining burst time. Q1 SRTF vs SJF Statement: "Shortest remaining time first (SRTF) behaves like shortest job first (SJF) if the set of processes is fixed (no new processes) and they are completely CPU-bound." Answer: True.. Why: SRTF is the preemptive version of SJF. If the process set is fixed (no new arrivals) and processes are CPU-bound (no I/O that causes intermediate blocking/arrivals), the preemption never happens in ways that change the SJF ordering - the schedules coincide. Q2 - CPU-bound vs I/O-bound and effect on context switches Difference (simple): CPU-bound process: does long CPU bursts, rarely issues I/O. It holds the CPU for long stretches. . I/O-bound process: does short CPU bursts and frequently does I/O (blocks often) Effect on context switching: I/O-bound processes cause more frequent context switches because they block for I/O and the OS must switch to another process often. They produce many short CPU bursts (more scheduler decisions) before switching). If preempted by time quantum, they still cause switches but less frequently than many small I/O bursts.. (So I/O-bound context-switch rate; CPU-bound context-switch frequency but each switch may cost the same overhead.) Q3 - Counting semaphore / one erroneous process. Q3 - Counting semaphore / one erroneous process. Problem setup (interpreted): Ten processes share a critical section using a counting semaphore x initially 1. Nine processes correctly do:. wait(x); CriticalSection; signal(x); One process erroneously does: signal(x); CriticalSection; signal(x); Question: What is the maximum number of processes that can be inside the critical section at the same time? Answer: 3 processes (maximum) Why (short): Start x = 1. The erroneous process does signa1(x) before entering -> x becomes 2 Then that erroneous process enters its critical section (without waiting). Meanwhile up to two of the correct processes can perform wait(x) while x is positive, decrementing it and entering the critical section. That gives at most: the erroneous process + two correct processes = 3 concurrently inside. The erroneous process's later signa1 happens after it leaves and cannot increase the simultaneous count at the same instant. Q4 - When is Rate-Monotonic (RM) inferior to Earliest-Deadline-First (EDF)? Answer (concise): RM is inferior when task sets have utilization or deadline patterns that EDF can schedule but RM cannot. Concretely: RM is static priority and has a schedulability bound: for n periodic tasks, guaranteed schedulability if total utilization U n(2^{1/n} - 1) (~ 69% as n ->). EDF (dynamic priority) can schedule many task sets with U up to 100% (in theory) that RM cannot. Also when deadlines are dynamic or deadlines periods or tasks are not strictly periodic, EDF's dynamic priorities give it an advantage. patterns where EDF can meet deadlines and RM cannot), EDF will succeed where RM fails. If yes how? If no, how to deal with starvation? Answer: Yes - detection and mitigation both possible. Detection: The system can monitor per-process waiting times (time spent ready but not scheduled) or number of times preempted. If a process's waiting time exceeds a configured threshold, the system can treat that as starvation. Mitigation (common technigues): Aging: gradually increase the priority of processes that wait long, so they eventually get CPU. Fair schedulers: use fair-share / proportional-share schedulers (CFS in Linux style) which bound starvation.. Admission / resource limits: limit how many high-priority tasks a user or class can have so low-priority tasks aren't starved. So detection is practical; the usual response is to apply aging or use a scheduler that guarantees progress. Code (interpreted from image): pid_t pid; pid = fork(); if (pid == O) { /* child process */ fork(); thread_create(...); fork(); (a) How many unique processes are created?. Answer: 6 processes total. After exiting. the if block, the final fork() is executed by all current processes (the original parent and both. child processes), doubling them from 3 -> 6.. (b) How many unique threads are created? Answer: 2 new threads (created by thread_create), assuming thread_create is executed in both the child and the child's child (both execute the code after the inner fork). So two threads are created; the total number of threads depends on whether the forks duplicate threads in a particular environment, but the direct thread_create invocations run twice -> two threads. (If the question asks "how many threads exist at the end?" you must also count the main. threads in each process - but typical exam wants "how many new threads created by. thread_create()" = 2.) In a finite cyclic group (G=\langle g\rangle) of known order (m), given (g) and (h\in G), the DLP is: find (x\in{0,\dots,m-1}) such that (g^x=h).. Algorithm better than exhaustive search. (Two standard choices.) Baby-step / Giant-step (Shanks). Let (m) be group order, set (m_0=\lceillsqrt m\rceil) Precompute baby steps ({g^j:0\le j<m_0}) in a table. Then compute (g^{-m_0 i} h) for (i=0,1,\dots) and Iook up matches. This finds (x=i m_0 + j) in time and memory (O(\sqrt m)). Pollard's Rho for Iogarithms. A randomised algorithm with expected time (O(\sqrt m)) and negligible memory. For multiplicative groups (large prime modulus) index-calculus methods can be faster in practice and subexponential in the prime size.. Zero-Knowledge proof of knowledge (based on DLP hardness).. A standard ZK protocol to prove knowledge of discrete log (x) for (h=g^x) is Schnorr's. protocol (3-move, honest-verifier ZK): 1. Prover picks random (r), sends commitment (t=g^r) 2. State Space Search for the Rabbit Leap Problem: BFS vs DFS Comparison Your Name,Student Member, IEEE Abstract—This report models the Rabbit Leap problem as a state space search problem and investigates solutions using Breadth First Search (BFS) and Depth First Search (DFS). BFS guarantees optimality in terms of the fewest moves, while DFS provides a feasible solution but may not always be optimal. A comparison of the two algorithms in terms of solution quality, time, and space complexity is presented. I. PROBLEMSTATEMENT In the Rabbit Leap problem, three east-bound rabbits stand in a line blocked by three west-bound rabbits. They are crossing a stream with stones placed in a line. There is one empty stone between them. The rabbits can move forward one or two steps. They can jump over one rabbit if needed, but not more than one at a time. The objective is to determine whether the rabbits can successfully cross each other without stepping into the water. II. STATESPACEMODELING Each state is represented as a string of length 7, e.g., EEE_WWW, where ‘E‘ denotes an east-moving rabbit, ‘W‘ denotes a west-moving rabbit, and ‘ ‘denotestheemptystone. Initial state:EEE_WWW Goal state:WWW_EEE At each step, the empty position can be swapped with an adjacent rabbit (1 step move) or a rabbit two positions away (jump). The total search space is7!/(3!3!1!) = 140 distinct configurations. III. BFS SOLUTION BFS explores all states level by level until the goal state is found. Since BFS explores in increasing depth, the first time it reaches the goal guarantees the minimum number of moves. 1from collections import deque 3def bfs(start, goal): 4visited = set([start]) 5queue = deque([[start]]) 6while queue: 7path = queue.popleft() 8state = path[-1] 9if state == goal: 10return path 11for nxt in generate_moves(state): 12if nxt not in visited: 13visited.add(nxt) 14queue.append(path + [nxt]) Listing 1. BFS Implementation in Python Result:BFS found a solution in 15 moves, which is the optimal solution length. IV. DFS

BFS Implementation in Python Result:BFS found a solution in 15 moves, which is the optimal solution length. IV. DFS SOLUTION DFS explores states by following a branch as deeply as possible before backtracking. Although it finds a valid solution, the path is not guaranteed to be optimal. 1def dfs(start, goal, visited=None, path=None) 2if visited is None: visited = set() 3if path is None: path = [] 4path.append(start) 5visited.add(start) 6if start == goal: 7return path 8for nxt in generate_moves(start): 9if nxt not in visited: 10res = dfs(nxt, goal, visited, path.copy()) 11if res: return res 12return None Listing 2. DFS Implementation in Python Result:DFS also produced a solution in 15 moves in one run, but this is not guaranteed. Depending on the move order, DFS could produce longer solutions. V. COMPARISON A. Solution Quality •BFS always produces the optimal (shortest) solution. •DFS may or may not produce the optimal solution. B. Time Complexity •BFS:O(b d), wherebis the branching factor andd is the depth of the optimal solution. •DFS:O(b m), wheremis the maximum depth of the search tree. C. Space Complexity •BFS:O(b d), since all nodes at each level are stored. •DFS:O(bm), storing only a single path and back- tracking stack. VI. CONCLUSION The Rabbit Leap problem can be solved optimally with BFS in 15 steps. DFS can also find a valid solution but may not guarantee optimality. BFS is more expensive in memory, while DFS is more memory efficient but less reliable in terms of solution quality. The experimental results confirm the theoretical trade-offs between the two algorithms. REFERENCES

Position of Two Circles d > r1+r2: 4 common tangents d = r1+r2: 3 common tangents |r1-r2| < d < r1+r2: 2 common tangents d = |r1-r2|: 1 common tangent d < |r1-r2|: 0 common tangents Length of Common Tangents Direct common tangent = √(d²-(r1-r2)²) Transverse common tangent = √(d²-(r1+r2)²) Orthogonal Circles Two circles intersect orthogonally when angle between tangents is 90°. For S1=x²+y²+2g1x+2f1y+c1=0 and S2=x²+y²+2g2x+2f2y+c2=0 Condition: 2(g1g2+f1f2)=c1+c2 Radical Axis Locus of points having equal tangent lengths to two circles. Equation: S−S' = 0 Properties of Radical Axis 1. For intersecting circles, common chord = radical axis. 2. For touching circles, common tangent at contact point = radical axis. 3. Radical axis is perpendicular to line joining centres. 4. Radical axis bisects common tangents. 5. Concentric circles have no radical axis. 6. If two circles cut a third circle orthogonally, their radical axis passes through centre of third circle. Radical Centre Radical axes of three circles intersect at one point called radical centre. LATEXTikZposter Shoplifting Detection in Surveillance Video Using YOLOv8: A Lightweight DL Pipeline Dev Saraswat (202351173) Satish Chauhan (202352330) Shivam Kumar (202351130) Department of Computer Science & Engineering, Indian Institute of Information Technology Vadodara Shoplifting Detection in Surveillance Video Using YOLOv8: A Lightweight DL Pipeline Dev Saraswat (202351173) Satish Chauhan (202352330) Shivam Kumar (202351130) Department of Computer Science & Engineering, Indian Institute of Information Technology Vadodara Abstract This project presents a lightweight, reproducible pipeline for detecting shoplifting behaviour in surveillance video using the classification variant of YOLOv8. The workflow includes frame extraction, dataset creation, model fine- tuning, per-frame inference, and temporal aggregation to generate robust video-level decisions. Objectives & Contributions •Reproducible end-to-end pipeline us- ing YOLOv8 (classification). •Frame-level classification & temporal aggregation for video-level decisions. •Empirical demonstration of improve- ment after fine-tuning (baseline→ fine-tuned). •Public scripts for data prep, training, testing and evaluation. System Overview (Pipeline) Five-stage pipeline: video→frames→frame classifier→aggregation→results. Dataset & Preprocessing Classes:Normal, Shoplifting. Stats: •Normal: 74 video folders (train). •Shoplifting: 40+ labeled video samples (train). •Up to 30 frames extracted per video (uni- form sampling). •Directory layout:dataset/- train/<class>/<video>/frame_*.jpg. a flexible respiration sensor is designed and embedded into a wearable strap to sense chest expansion and contraction during breathing. The sensor output is processed using an embedded microcontroller that performs data acquisition and preprocessing. Machine Iearning algorithms are then applied to analyze the breathing signal and extract meaningful information such as respiratory rate and breathing patterns. The system emphasizes comfort, portability, low power consumption, and intelligent data analysis. Implementation Details. 1. Sensor Design and Fabrication. A flexible piezoresistive/stretch sensor is mounted on an elastic chest strap.. Chest movement during breathing causes the sensor to stretch. 2. Signal Conditioning. Analog filters are applied to remove noise and motion artifacts.. The conditioned signal represents the breathing waveform. 3. Embedded Data Acquisition The conditioned analog signal is sampled using the ADC of a microcontroller.. Sampling rate is maintained at 25-50 Hz, sufficient for respiration signals. Digital preprocessing such as filtering and normalization is performed. 4. Machine Learning Analytics. Preprocessed data is segmented into fixed time windows. A lightweight machine learning model (1D-CNN or LSTM) is trained.. The model estimates respiratory rate and classifies breathing patterns (normal, fast, shallow, apnea).. 5. Output and Communication. The results are displayed as breaths per minute (BPM). Data can be transmitted wirelessly to a mobile or PC application for visualization and storage. Tools Used. Traditional underwater communication, like acoustics and radio waves, performs poorly in. shallow water because of multipath, noise, and high attenuation. reflections like. acoustic waves. This gives MI systems predictable behavior, low attenuation, and even covert operation because the electric radiation is minimal.. 3. System Architecture The MI transmitter includes a high-Q coil, a resonant driver, and sometimes a magnetic core to boost performance.e The receiver uses a multi-turn coil, a resonance circuit, and a low-noise amplifier to detect very weak magnetic signals. Together, these components allow reliable communication in harsh littoral conditions. 4. Modulation MI systems usually use ASK, FSK, PSK, or MSK modulation. At frequencies below 6 kHz, we typically achieve 10 to 500 bps, which is suitable for underwater sensing, short messages, and navigation. 5. Historical Experiments Several important field tests established the reliability of Ml communication. In 1997, signals were successfully transmitted over more than 1300 yards in shallow water. In 1998, seabed tests showed ranges beyond 300 meters, and in 2001 UBV-sized systems achieved 16-300 bps over 250-400 meters. 6. Major Advances (2005-2025) Recent research introduced compact coil designs, metamaterial resonators, relay-aided networks, and MI waveguides made of passive resonant loops. MI is also used for underwater localization, achieving 0.5 to 1 meter accuracy, even where acoustic systems fail. 7. Challenges Despite its advantages, MI has limitations such as 1/R3 signal decay, narrow bandwidth, and sensitivity to coil orientation. Environmental conductivity also affects performance. 8. Conclusion In summary, MI communication offers a stable, predictable, and energy-efficient solution for shallow-water and mixed-media environments. With recent improvements, it is becoming a key technology for underwater networks, autonomous systems, and future littoral communication applications. Assignment Report: TinyLinux Minimal. Tail latency spikes, especially at the 95th, 99th, and 99.9th percentiles, can degrade application responsiveness. This project leverages Performance Monitoring Unit (Pmu) counters to correlate hardware-level metrics with latency behavior. Using the Linux perf tool and perf_event_open syscall, we collected and analyzed CPU performance events. Results indicate strong correlations between branch mispredictions, CPI, IPC, bus cycles, and latency. Introduction Modern systems demand consistent performance, but latency spikes are common under high Ioad. Identifying their root cause requires observability at the hardware level. PMU counters provide low-level metrics (cycles, instructions, cache misses, bus cycles, branch mispredictions) which can reveal inefficiencies. Our goal was to monitor PMU counters in real time, correlate them with workload latency, and visualize the impact of microarchitectural behavior on system performance. Problem Statement: How can PmU counters be used to detect and analyze latency spikes in modern processors? Objective: Capture PMU counters programmatically. Correlate with latency traces. Identify counters linked with p95/p99/p999 latency. Prototype a lightweight monitoring framework.. Tail Latency Research: Prior research (Treadmill, NSDl) highlights importance of tail. performance in cloud systems. Perf Tools: Linux perf widely used for profiling, though not tailored for tail latency monitoring. Our project bridges these by directly correlating latency with PMU metrics.. o Use of magnetic core (enhances effective permeability E) RMS induced voltage: (V = \mu_E 2 \pi B A f N) High-Q coil output voltage enhanced by factor Q, improves matching with. amplifier.Here's a simplified explanation of Modulation & Data Transmission in MI. systems: Modulating the AC Magnetic Field The AC magnetic field generated by the transmitter can be varied to carry information. Common modulation schemes: Frequency Shift Keying (FSK), Phase Shift Keying (PSK), Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK), and Minimum Shift Keying (MSK).. This allows binary or analog data to be sent over the magnetic field. Bandwidth and Data Rate. Typical MI system bandwidth: 1 Hz to 600 Hz, though higher data rates are possible with careful design. Data rate is limited by the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the receiver - weaker signals or higher noise reduce reliable throughput. Receiver Sensitivity Factors Antenna (coil) cross-section (A): Larger area collects more magnetic flux. Number of turns (N): More turns increase induced voltage . Methodology Implemented: Al-Based Space Object Detection & Collision Prediction Framework Complete Detailed Project Roadmap (VR Removed) H EXECUTIVE SUMMARY Project Name: ASOD-CPF (Al-Based Space Object Detection and Collision Prediction Framework) Problem: Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite populations are exploding from approximately 9,000 in 2025 to over 50,000 by 2030. Traditional pairwise collision analysis has O(n?) complexity, making it computationally impossible at this scale. Small debris remains difficult to detect, and deterministic distance thresholds ignore inherent TLE (Two-Line Element) uncertainty. Solution: A complete software-only system combining SGP4 orbit propagation, Graph Neural uncertainty quantification using 1,000+ samples per satellite pair, and a production-ready 3D interactive web dashboard with real-time alerts. Key Innovation: GNN replaces traditional O(n?) pairwise analysis with O(n) sparse graph approach, enabling scalability to 50,o00+ objects while maintaining high accuracy Impact: Directly supports LEO safety for megaconstellations, aligns with India's Space Situational Awareness (SSA) needs, and is highly publishable with proven metrics (mAP50 0.75, 7-day advance prediction). Feasibility: Fully software-only with zero hardware requirements, uses free open data from CelesTrak, leverages mature libraries (SGP4, PyTorch Geometric), and has a concrete 4-week implementation roadmap. Total Cost: $50-600 (primarily cloud GPU for ML training, no hardware needed) DETAILED OBJECTIVES Heart Disease Detection System Project Report Date: 02-09-2025 Author: Shivam Kumar (ML INTERN) Company: Unified Mentor Pvt. A deep learning model based on Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is proposed to predict commodity prices and help MSMEs make informed decisions. The model used multivariate time series data collected from PIHPS Nasional and includes features like rice, chicken meat, eggs, and several types of chilies. Key Achievements:. Best MAE: 255.998 Orbit Propagation Module Algorithm: SGP4 (Simple General Perturbation 4) and SDP4 for deep space Libraries: Python sgp4, astropy, skyfield Output: Earth-Centered Inertial (ECl) coordinates (x, y, z in km) and velocity (vx, vy, vz. in km/s) Performance: Can propagate 10,000 satellites in under 1 second Conjunction Engine Module. Two-Stage Filtering: 1. Coarse filter: Distance threshold 10 km (eliminates 99% of pairs, O(n). complexity) 2. Precise filter: Relative velocity >5 km/s, altitude difference <50 km. Min Separation Calculation: Numerical integration using Runge-Kutta 4 method with gradient-based optimization. Collision Probability: Monte Carlo with 1,000 samples, 3o covariance ellipses. Risk LeveIs: LOW (<0.1), MEDIUM (0.1-0.3), HIGH (0.3-0.7), CRITICAL (>0.7) Uncertainty Quantification Module. . Monte Carlo Pipeline: 1. Sample TLE uncertainty: Bstar drag term 5%, mean motion +0.0001 rev/day, inclination 0.001 2. Propagate 1,000 samples using SGP4 for each satellite 3. Compute covariance matrix (33) from position samples 4. Extract 3o ellipse radii from eigenvalues 5. Collision if distance between means < combined 3o radius Output: Mean position, covariance matrix, ellipse radii, collision probability. Layer 3: ML Layer (Detailed). Introduction With the rapid advancement of Industry 4.0, smart cities, and cyber-physical systems, Digital Twins have become a foundational technology for representing, monitoring, and optimizing real-world systems in virtual environments. A digital twin establishes a continuous, bidirectional connection between a physical system and its virtual counterpart, enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and decision support. However, most existing digital twin platforms rely on 2D dashboards or static 3D CAD. models, which are insufficient for understanding complex spatial structures, dense. environments, and dynamic system behavior. These limitations reduce situational awareness At the same time, Knowledge Graphs (KGs) provide a structured semantic representation of entities, attributes, and relationships, enabling intelligent querying, reasoning, and inference. diagrams, disconnected from real-world geometry.. Meanwhile, modern sensing technologies such as LiDAR and photogrammetry generate dense 3D point cloud data, offering highly accurate geometric representations of real environments. However, interacting with massive point clouds using traditional interfaces is computationally expensive and cognitively challenging. This project proposes an Immersive Digital Twin Platform that integrates Knowledge Graph reasoning with real-time 3D point cloud visualization inside VR/AR environments, enabling intuitive, semantic, and spatial interaction with complex digital twins.. 2. Core Problem Definition (What Exactly Is the Problem?). Despite advances in digital twin technology, three fundamental problems remain unresolved: 1. Geometry-Semantics Disconnection Geometric data (3D models, point clouds) and semantic data (knowledge graphs, rules) 2. Non-Immersive Interaction Traditional 2D dashboards and flat 3D views fail to convey spatial complexity, reducing human understanding and increasing cognitive load. 3. Scalability & Performance Challenges Real-time rendering and interaction with large-scale point cloud data is computationally expensive and poorly supported in immersive systems. These issues significantly limit the practical use of digital twins in smart manufacturing, infrastructure monitoring, smart cities, and cyber-physical systems. 3. Problem Statement Traditional digital twin systems suffer from: Limited spatial understanding

Static Binaries o Programs like nano and cur1 are added as standalone static binaries to provide basic editing and network functionality o Using static binaries avoids dependency issues, keeping the system minimal. 3. Custom Init Script o init is the entry point for the tiny Linux system. oResponsible for: Mounting proc and sysfs o Include standalone versions of nano, curl, or other essential tools. 4. The study addresses the challenges faced by Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) due to fluctuating commodity prices. A multivariate LSTM model with the Adam optimizer and Batch Normalization demonstrates superior performance, achieving low Mean Absolute Error (MAE) while reducing overfitting and underfitting risks. We compare several LSTM variants and hybrid architectures, provide implementation de- tails, hyperparameter choices, and reproducible artifacts. Index Terms—time series forecasting, LSTM, attention, multi- variate forecasting, commodity prices, India I. INTRODUCTION A. Time Series Prediction Time series prediction involves the analysis of sequential data to forecast future values. It is widely applied in predicting stock prices, commodity prices, and other temporal phenom- ena. B. Background and Problem Statement During the COVID-19 pandemic, many MSMEs struggled due to: •Lack of crisis planning skills. •Fluctuating commodity prices leading to temporary busi- ness closures. •Declining turnover. 1) COVID-19 Impact on MSMEs:The pandemic disrupted supply chains and demand, causing drastic commodity price fluctuations. 2) Russia–Ukraine Conflict and Oil Price Volatility:Since 2022 the Russia–Ukraine conflict increased volatility in oil and grain markets, raising transport and production costs. 3) Climate Change and Extreme Weather:Extreme weather events have increased supply shocks for agricultural commodi- ties. 4) India’s Agricultural Price Instability:Staple commodi- ties such as onions, tomatoes, and pulses often experience sudden swings due to seasonality, supply inefficiencies, and policy interventions. C. Proposed Solution We develop a multivariate LSTM-based forecasting frame- work (and variants) to provide early warning and stabilize MSME decision-making. CONCLUSION This study successfully developed and evaluated multiple LSTM-based architectures for forecasting commodity prices across 11 essential commodities in India. Using 1,460 daily records, we demonstrated that the Attention LSTM model achieved superior performance with a validation loss of 0.018162, MAE of 0.027, RMSE of 0.039, andR 2 of 0.91, outperforming standard LSTM, Bi-LSTM, and Conv-LSTM variants. The model was trained for up to 200 epochs using the Adam optimizer with adaptive learning rate schedul- ing, effectively capturing complex temporal dependencies and inter-commodity correlations. Key contributions include: (1) a comprehensive comparison of LSTM variants on multivari- ate commodity price data, (2) robust training methodology with Batch Normalization and gradient clipping to prevent overfitting, and (3) empirical validation demonstrating that attention mechanisms significantly improve prediction accu- racy for multidimensional time series. The framework provides MSMEs with actionable early-warning capabilities to antici- pate price fluctuations during volatile market conditions such as pandemics, geopolitical conflicts, and climate events. Future work will explore Transformer-based architectures for longer forecasting horizons and incorporate exogenous variables such as weather patterns, policy changes, and global market indi- cators to further enhance predictive accuracy. Flexible Respiration Sensor with Embedded Data Acquisition and Machine Learning-based Analytics Mohli Thapa Computer Science & Engineering Indian Institute of Information Technology Vadodara Gandhinagar, Gujarat 202351084@iiitvadodara.ac.in SBND Abhijeet Computer Science & Engineering Indian Institute of Information Technology Vadodara Gandhinagar, Gujarat 202351127@iiitvadodara.ac.in Keshav Badaya Computer Science & Engineering Indian Institute of Information Technology Vadodara Gandhinagar, Gujarat 202351065@iiitvadodara.ac.in Shivam Kumar Computer Science & Engineering Indian Institute of Information Technology Vadodara Gandhinagar, Gujarat 202351130@iiitvadodara.ac.in Kamal Kishor Jha Electronics & Communication Engineering Indian Institute of Information Technology Vadodara Gandhinagar, Gujarat kamal@iiitvadodara.ac.in Abstract—Respiration monitoring is crucial in the field of human healthcare as it impacts various health conditions includ- ing sleeping disorders like Apnea and respiratory diseases. Old respiratory monitoring techniques like chest or abdomen belts and nasal probes are uncomfortable to wear and expensive as well. Thus, there is a requirement for a low cost and flexible sensing system for continuous monitoring of breathing. This paper describes the fabrication and working of a humidity- based resistive sensor, designed to have an interdigitated electrode structure. Breathing data is collected through an embedded data acquisition system consisting of voltage divider circuitry, LM358 unity-gain buffer and ESP32 microcontroller’s ADC. Collected breathing signal data (a total of 30,000 samples) is passed through a machine learning pipeline which includes data labeling, noise removal using low pass filters, feature extraction, stratified splitting of dataset and feature scaling. A random forest classifier is trained and tested, resulting in an accuracy of 97.3% approximately, in classifying into shallow, normal and deep breathing patterns. Amplitude, maximum value and minimum value features are found to be the most important features in classification. The fabricated sensor showed a response time of 1.9 seconds and a hysteresis error of 10.17% in the characterization plots. The proposed system in this study is effective in inputting and analysing breathing signals using machine learning which can further help in the diagnosis of sleep apnea and other related health conditions.

The continuous de- crease in sensor’s resistance intially can be explained from the fact that with increase in temperature, kinetic energy of charge carriers on the sensor’s surface increases, leading to decrease in resistance. II. EXPERIMENTALSETUP A. Design and Fabrication of the Sensor The flexible resistive humidity sensor [31] was designed in the form of an interdigitated pattern of 27 interlocking fingers arranged between 2 vertical electrode bars. Width of each finger was set to 0.1 mm and the spacing between every two consecutive fingers was set to 0.7 mm. The electrodes’ width was also set to 0.1 mm and the spacing between them was kept as 1 cm. The active sensing area of the designed sensor is approximately 2cm 2 and its sensitivity to moisture has been increased by populating the design with a high number of electrical fingers. The max overall length of the humidity sensor is 3.5 cm and overall width is 1.4 cm. The corners of the rectangle shaped-sensor were kept rounded so as to ensure that the corners don’t get stuck inside clothing when worn. Figure 1(a) displays the design of the resistive humdity sensor. Fabrication process of this sensor involved printing the de- signed humidity sensor on a polyimide based flexible substrate, as shown in Figure 2. The flexible substrate was deposited with a moisture sensitive material. The changes in humidity concentrations directly cause variations in the resistance of the sensor. The fully fabricated sensor has been shown in Figure 1(c). When a person inhales and exhales in front of the sensor (Fig 1(b)), the humidity concentrations near the sensor vary slightly, leading to measurable resistance changes. The design of the sensor is optimal as moisture in the exhaled breath gets settled down on the gaps between the fingers, changing the conductivity of the

Humidity concentrations are dis- cretely changed from 0% to 100% during exhalation & 100% to 0% during recovery. The up cycle shows the loading effect (exhalation) of the sensor in which its resistance increases with increasing humidity concentration. During exhaling, moisture droplets deposit on the sensor’s surface, increasing the re- sistance value, while the resistance value decreases during the recovery phase (unloading effect) which represents the down cycle in the graph. Calculation of hysteresis error for this hysteresis curve gives a 10.17% error which shows good repeatability of sensor but due to some minute moisture droplets which get retained in the gaps on the sensor surface, some lag has been observed. The second characterization plot is about the measurement of the fabricated sensor’s response time, as shown in Figure 4. The sensor’s response time [35] is defined as the amount of time the sensor takes to reach 90% of its max voltage (V max) from its baseline voltage value, during exhalation process. Sensor was kept in a stable environment for a while to record its baseline voltage value. Deep exhalation was then performed starting atT start to record how the sensor’s resistance & output voltage slowly increases to its maximum value. 90% of the max voltage was calculated & the time (T 90%) was Fig. 3. Hysteresis curve plot of the resistive sensor. noted at which the sensor achieves 90%V max. And thus, the calculated response time came out to be nearly 1.9 seconds which is sufficiently fast to detect breathing cycles of a healthy adult at resting condition (full breaths last for 4 to 5 seconds). The internet has made it possible to talk to people anywhere in the world instantly. Social media lets us share personal moments, opinions, and where people with similar interests can connect and support each other. However, with these benefits come challenges like privacy issues, misinformation, and digital surveillance. UNIT–1: UNITS AND DIMENSIONS 1. Physical Quantity Definition A physical quantity is any quantity that can be measured. Examples: Length Mass Time Temperature Force Energy Physical quantities are divided into two categories: (A) Fundamental Quantities These quantities are independent and do not depend on any other physical quantity. Examples: 1. Length 2. Mass 3. Time 4. Temperature 5. Electric Current 6. Luminous Intensity 7. Amount of Substance (B) Derived Quantities These quantities depend upon one or more fundamental quantities. Examples: Area Area = Length × Breadth Dimension: [L²] Speed Speed = Distance / Time Dimension: [LT⁻¹] Volume Volume = Length × Breadth × Height Dimension: [L³] 2. Unit Definition A unit is the physical standard by which a physical quantity is measured. Examples: metre (m) kilogram (kg) second (s) (B) Derived Units Units corresponding to derived quantities. Examples: Newton Joule Pascal Watt 3. Measurement Measurement is the process of comparing an unknown physical quantity with a standard quantity of the same nature. Examples: Measuring length using a meter scale. Measuring mass using a balance. Measuring time using a clock. The result of a measurement is expressed as: Measured Quantity = Numerical Value × Unit Example: Length = 5 m where: 5 = numerical value m = unit 2. Need for Measurement Measurement is necessary because: It allows comparison of physical quantities. It helps perform scientific experiments. It enables mathematical calculations. It provides quantitative information about physical phenomena. 3. Accuracy and Precision Accuracy Accuracy refers to how close a measured value is to the true value. Example: True value = 10.0 cm Measured value = 9.98 cm The measurement is highly accurate. Precision Precision refers to the degree of agreement among repeated measurements. Example: 10.01 cm 10.02 cm 1. 2. 3. 4. 10.01 cm 10.02 cm These readings are highly precise. Difference Between Accuracy and Precision Accuracy Precision Near true value Near each other Related to correctness Related to consistency Affected by systematic errorsAffected by random errors 4. Error in Measurement Every measurement contains some uncertainty. The difference between the measured value and true value is called error. Error = True Value − Measured Value Symbolically, E = T − M where: E = Error T = True Value M = Measured Value 5. Causes of Errors Errors arise due to: Instrument defects Observer mistakes Environmental conditions Experimental limitations 6. Classification of Errors Errors are mainly classified into: Systematic Errors Random Errors 7. Systematic Errors Systematic errors are errors whose causes are known and can usually be corrected. Finding the arithmetic mean. 1. 2. 9. Mean (True) Value Suppose a quantity is measured n times. Observations: a₁, a₂, a₃,...., aₙ The mean value is: aₘ = (a₁ + a₂ + a₃ +... + aₙ)/n This mean value is considered the most probable value of the measurement. Absolute Error The absolute error for each observation is the numerical difference between the mean value and the observed value. Triangle Law of Vector Addition Statement If two vectors are represented in magnitude and direction by two sides of a triangle taken in the same order, then the third side taken in the opposite order represents their resultant. Understanding the Statement Suppose: A⃗ and B⃗ are two vectors. Place the tail of B⃗ at the head of A⃗. The vector joining the starting point of A⃗ to the ending point of B⃗ gives the resultant. Geometrical Construction Step 1: Draw vector A⃗ Step 2: From the head of A⃗ draw vector B⃗ Step 3: Join the initial point of A⃗ to the final point of B⃗. This line represents resultant: R⃗ Mathematical Representation R⃗ = A⃗ + B⃗ Why Triangle Law Works The two vectors form two sides of a triangle. The third side naturally represents their combined effect. Hence resultant is obtained geometrically. 4. Magnitude of Resultant Vector Suppose: Magnitude of first vector = A Magnitude of second vector = B Angle between them = θ Resultant magnitude = R Derivation Using Cosine Rule From triangle geometry: R² = A² + B² + 2AB cosθ Taking square root: This is the fundamental formula of vector addition. This chapter covers: Parallelogram Law Resultant Vector Resolution of Vectors Rectangular Components Unit Vector Representation Coordinate Form of Vectors Magnitude of a Vector Applications 1. Parallelogram Law of Vector Addition Statement If two vectors acting simultaneously on a particle are represented in magnitude and direction by two adjacent sides of a parallelogram drawn from a common point, then the diagonal passing through that common point represents the resultant vector. Explanation Suppose two vectors A⃗ and B⃗ originate from the same point O. A parallelogram is constructed using these vectors as adjacent sides. The diagonal passing through O gives the resultant vector. Construction Step 1 Draw vector A⃗. Step 2 Draw vector B⃗ from the same origin. Step 3 Complete the parallelogram. Step 4 Draw the diagonal through O. This diagonal represents the resultant. Mathematical Representation R⃗ = A⃗ + B⃗ Importance Parallelogram law provides a graphical method of adding vectors acting at a common point. Amount of Substance Supplementary Units: Radian Steradian Dimensional Analysis Applications: Checking equations Unit conversion Deriving relations Most Important Formula: F = ma Dimension: [MLT⁻²] 1. 2. 3. 10 UNIT–1: UNITS AND DIMENSIONS PART – 4 ADVANCED DIMENSIONAL ANALYSIS NOTES LIMITATIONS OF DIMENSIONAL ANALYSIS Although dimensional analysis is a powerful tool, it has some limitations. 1. Cannot Determine Numerical Constants Dimensional analysis cannot determine numerical constants such as: 1/2 Example: Area of circle A = πr² Using dimensions we can find that area is proportional to r² but cannot determine the value of π. 2. Cannot Distinguish Between Similar Quantities Many physical quantities possess the same dimensional formula. Examples: Work Dimension: [ML²T⁻²] Energy Dimension: [ML²T⁻²] Torque Dimension: [ML²T⁻²] All have the same dimensions but different physical meanings. 3. Cannot Derive Trigonometric Functions Dimensional analysis cannot establish equations involving: sinθ cosθ tanθ logarithms exponential functions Example: y = A sin(ωt) cannot be derived completely using dimensions. 4. Cannot Check Vector or Scalar Nature Dimensions only provide magnitude information. They do not indicate whether a quantity is: Scalar Vector Example: Work and Torque possess identical dimensions but differ in nature. COMMONLY USED PHYSICAL QUANTITIES Force Definition: Force is an external agency that changes or attempts to change the state of motion of a body. Formula: F = ma SI Unit: Newton (N) Dimension: [MLT⁻²] Work Definition: Work is said to be done when force causes displacement. Formula: W = Fs SI Unit: Joule (J) Dimension: [ML²T⁻²] Energy Definition: Capacity to perform work. SI Unit: Joule Dimension: [ML²T⁻²] Power Definition: Rate of doing work. Formula: P = W/t SI Unit: Watt Dimension: [ML²T⁻³] Pressure Definition: Force acting per unit area. Formula: P = F/A
